levels.title: Query Writer
levels.summary: Reads data from single tables using basic SELECT, WHERE, and ORDER BY. Treats SQL as a lookup tool.
definition: The Query Writer can retrieve data from a database using a GUI or command-line client. They write single-table queries, apply simple filters, and sort results.
At this level, SQL is useful but results are unpredictable. The practitioner does not think about NULLs, duplicates, or performance.

coreConcepts.name: SELECT Basics
coreConcepts.definition: Understanding how to retrieve columns from a single table using SELECT, WHERE, and ORDER BY.

coreConcepts.name: Data Types
coreConcepts.definition: Awareness that columns have types (VARCHAR, INTEGER, DATE) and that type mismatches cause errors.

coreConcepts.name: NULL Awareness
coreConcepts.definition: Basic understanding that NULL means 'unknown' and behaves differently from empty strings or zero.

coreConcepts.name: Row Filtering
coreConcepts.definition: Using WHERE clauses with comparison operators, LIKE, IN, and BETWEEN.

coreConcepts.name: Result Sorting
coreConcepts.definition: Using ORDER BY to control the sequence of returned rows.

coreSkills.name: Basic SELECT
coreSkills.definition: Can write a SELECT statement that returns specific columns from a table.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Writes SELECT col1, col2 FROM table rather than SELECT *.

coreSkills.name: WHERE Filtering
coreSkills.definition: Can filter rows using WHERE with basic operators.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Applies WHERE conditions to narrow result sets.

coreSkills.name: Result Sorting
coreSkills.definition: Can sort query results using ORDER BY.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Adds ORDER BY to queries that need deterministic output order.

coreSkills.name: SQL Client Usage
coreSkills.definition: Can connect to a database and execute queries using a GUI or CLI client.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Opens a SQL client and runs queries without external help.

coreSkills.name: Error Reading
coreSkills.definition: Can read a SQL error message and understand what went wrong.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Fixes syntax errors after reading the error message.

observableBehaviors: Opens a SQL client and types SELECT * FROM table to explore data.
Adds WHERE clauses by trial and error rather than intentional filter design.
Does not think about NULLs, duplicates, or row counts.
Cannot explain why a query returns zero rows.
failureModes: Uses SELECT * in production code.
Ignores NULL values and gets wrong aggregate results.
Does not check row counts to validate query correctness.
Cannot join two tables without help.

graduationCriteria.text: Writes correct multi-table INNER JOINs without assistance.
Uses GROUP BY with at least one aggregate function accurately.
Can explain the difference between WHERE and HAVING.
Writes basic INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE statements safely.

evaluationTasks.title: Write a Filtered Query
evaluationTasks.description: Write a query that returns customer names and order dates for orders placed in the last 30 days, sorted by date descending.
successCriteria: Uses explicit column names, not SELECT *.
WHERE clause correctly filters by date.
Results sorted by order date descending.

learningEmphasis: Practice writing queries against real datasets.
Learn to read and interpret error messages.
Explore documentation for your SQL client.

levels.title: Data Analyst
levels.summary: Constructs multi-table JOINs, aggregations, subqueries, and basic window functions for structured data analysis.
definition: The Data Analyst uses SQL as a primary analysis tool. They can answer non-trivial business questions from a relational database.
They write queries other people can read, handle NULLs and duplicates, use CTEs for organization, and write basic window functions.

coreConcepts.name: JOIN Types
coreConcepts.definition: Understanding INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, and CROSS JOINs and when to use each.

coreConcepts.name: Aggregation
coreConcepts.definition: Using GROUP BY with aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX) and HAVING.

coreConcepts.name: Subqueries
coreConcepts.definition: Writing subqueries in WHERE, FROM, and SELECT clauses.

coreConcepts.name: CTEs
coreConcepts.definition: Using Common Table Expressions (WITH clauses) to organize complex queries.

coreConcepts.name: Window Function Basics
coreConcepts.definition: Using ROW_NUMBER, RANK, and basic OVER clauses for analytical queries.

coreSkills.name: Multi-Table JOINs
coreSkills.definition: Can join three or more tables correctly.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Writes a query joining orders, customers, and products.

coreSkills.name: Aggregation Queries
coreSkills.definition: Can group and aggregate data to answer business questions.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Computes monthly revenue totals with GROUP BY.

coreSkills.name: CTE Organization
coreSkills.definition: Uses CTEs instead of nested subqueries for readability.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Restructures a nested query into CTEs when it exceeds two levels.

coreSkills.name: NULL Handling
coreSkills.definition: Handles NULLs deliberately using COALESCE, IS NULL, and NULL-safe comparisons.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Uses COALESCE in aggregations to avoid incorrect results.

coreSkills.name: DML Operations
coreSkills.definition: Can write safe INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE statements.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Adds WHERE clause to every UPDATE/DELETE and verifies row counts.

observableBehaviors: Structures queries with CTEs instead of deeply nested subqueries.
Chooses between LEFT and INNER JOIN deliberately.
Checks row counts before and after joins to detect fan-out.
Formats SQL consistently for readability.
failureModes: Creates accidental row multiplication from incorrect joins.
Uses GROUP BY but includes non-aggregated columns incorrectly.
Writes window functions without understanding PARTITION BY scope.
Does not validate query results against known data.

graduationCriteria.text: Designs a simple normalized schema (3NF) from requirements.
Writes and reasons about transactions with explicit COMMIT/ROLLBACK.
Reads a basic query execution plan and identifies a missing index.
Writes a stored procedure or function in at least one dialect.

evaluationTasks.title: Write an Analytical Report Query
evaluationTasks.description: Write a query that shows the top 5 customers by total spending in each product category, using window functions.
successCriteria: Correctly joins customers, orders, and products.
Uses window functions to rank within categories.

learningEmphasis: Practice complex joins on real schemas.

levels.title: Schema Designer
levels.summary: Designs normalized relational schemas, manages data integrity through constraints and transactions, and reads execution plans.
definition: The Schema Designer owns the structural layer of a database. They design tables from requirements rather than retrofitting queries onto ad-hoc schemas.
They enforce integrity via constraints, understand indexing write implications, and can diagnose common performance problems at the plan level.

coreConcepts.name: Normalization
coreConcepts.definition: Understanding 1NF through 3NF and when to deviate with documented reasoning.

coreConcepts.name: Constraints
coreConcepts.definition: Using NOT NULL, UNIQUE, CHECK, and FOREIGN KEY constraints to enforce data integrity.

coreConcepts.name: Transactions
coreConcepts.definition: Understanding ACID properties and using explicit transaction blocks with COMMIT/ROLLBACK.

coreConcepts.name: Execution Plans
coreConcepts.definition: Reading EXPLAIN output to understand how the database executes a query.

coreConcepts.name: Stored Procedures
coreConcepts.definition: Writing reusable server-side logic as stored procedures, functions, and views.

coreSkills.name: Schema Design
coreSkills.definition: Can design a normalized schema from business requirements.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Produces an ER diagram and DDL from a feature specification.

coreSkills.name: Migration Writing
coreSkills.definition: Can write reversible migration scripts.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Writes up/down migration scripts that can be rolled back safely.

coreSkills.name: Constraint Application
coreSkills.definition: Applies constraints systematically to enforce data integrity.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Every new table includes appropriate NOT NULL, FK, and CHECK constraints.

coreSkills.name: Execution Plan Reading
coreSkills.definition: Can read EXPLAIN output and identify seq scans vs index scans.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Identifies a missing index from a sequential scan in the plan.

coreSkills.name: Transaction Management
coreSkills.definition: Uses explicit transactions for multi-statement operations.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Wraps related DML statements in BEGIN/COMMIT blocks.

observableBehaviors: Normalizes to 3NF by default; deviates intentionally with documented reasoning.
Writes migration scripts that are reversible.
Adds foreign key constraints and understands their locking implications.
Reads EXPLAIN output and identifies seq scans vs index scans.
failureModes: Over-normalizes schemas that need read performance.
Writes migrations without considering rollback.
Adds indexes without understanding write overhead.
Uses stored procedures where application logic would be more maintainable.

graduationCriteria.text: Designs and validates an index strategy for a given query workload.
Demonstrates understanding of all four isolation levels with concrete failure scenarios.
Diagnoses and fixes a slow query using execution plan analysis.
Writes a partitioned table definition with appropriate partition key reasoning.

evaluationTasks.title: Design a Schema from Requirements
evaluationTasks.description: Given a feature specification for an e-commerce returns system, design a normalized schema with appropriate constraints and write migration DDL.
successCriteria: Schema is in 3NF or has documented reasons for denormalization.
All foreign keys and constraints are present.
Migration includes both up and down scripts.

learningEmphasis: Study normalization theory with real examples.

levels.title: Performance Engineer
levels.summary: Designs index strategies, interprets execution plans, optimizes slow queries, and manages concurrency and isolation.
definition: The Performance Engineer treats the database as a system to be tuned, not just queried. They understand statistics, cardinality, and join algorithms.
They design composite indexes deliberately, recognize implicit type-cast index suppression, and manage concurrency through isolation level selection.

coreConcepts.name: Index Strategy
coreConcepts.definition: Designing composite and covering indexes based on query workload analysis.

coreConcepts.name: Query Optimizer
coreConcepts.definition: Understanding how the optimizer selects plans based on statistics and cardinality estimates.

coreConcepts.name: Isolation Levels
coreConcepts.definition: Deep understanding of READ UNCOMMITTED through SERIALIZABLE and their concurrency implications.

coreConcepts.name: Partitioning
coreConcepts.definition: Table partitioning strategies for large datasets: range, list, and hash partitioning.

coreConcepts.name: Concurrency Control
coreConcepts.definition: Understanding MVCC, locking, deadlock detection, and lock granularity.

coreSkills.name: Index Design
coreSkills.definition: Can design optimal indexes for a given workload.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Analyzes slow query log and creates composite indexes that eliminate table scans.

coreSkills.name: Plan Optimization
coreSkills.definition: Can rewrite queries to produce better execution plans.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Rewrites a correlated subquery as a join when plan shows nested loops at scale.

coreSkills.name: Concurrency Management
coreSkills.definition: Selects appropriate isolation levels and locking strategies.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Chooses isolation level based on consistency requirements vs throughput trade-offs.

coreSkills.name: Partition Design
coreSkills.definition: Can partition large tables with appropriate partition keys.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Designs time-based partitioning for a high-volume event table.

coreSkills.name: Performance Diagnosis
coreSkills.definition: Can diagnose slow queries systematically.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Uses execution plans, statistics, and slow query logs together.

observableBehaviors: Reviews auto-generated indexes critically and removes unused ones.
Rewrites correlated subqueries as joins when the plan shows nested loops at scale.
Uses query hints only as a last resort with documented justification.
Understands MVCC mechanics and their impact on vacuum and replication lag.
failureModes: Over-indexes tables without measuring write impact.
Applies query hints as a first resort instead of fixing root causes.
Chooses SERIALIZABLE isolation level universally without considering trade-offs.
Optimizes queries without understanding the full workload pattern.

graduationCriteria.text: Designs a multi-tenant or partitioned architecture with horizontal scaling reasoning.
Defines an HA/DR strategy with RPO/RTO targets and replication configuration.
Authors SQL standards and review guidelines for a development team.
Evaluates SQL vs NoSQL trade-offs for a given workload and documents the decision.

evaluationTasks.title: Diagnose and Fix a Slow Query
evaluationTasks.description: Given a slow query execution plan and table statistics, identify the performance bottleneck, propose an optimization strategy, and validate the improvement.
successCriteria: Correctly identifies the bottleneck from the execution plan.
Proposed index or rewrite addresses the root cause.
Validates improvement with before/after plan comparison.

learningEmphasis: Practice with real-world slow query examples.
Learn MVCC and concurrency mechanics deeply.

levels.title: Database Architect
levels.summary: Designs enterprise data architectures, establishes governance and standards, and aligns data strategy across teams.
definition: The Database Architect operates at the organizational level. They design for scale, resilience, and team autonomy simultaneously.
They define data contracts between services, govern naming conventions and review processes, and make buy-vs-build decisions for data infrastructure.

coreConcepts.name: Distributed SQL
coreConcepts.definition: Understanding distributed database architectures, sharding strategies, and consistency trade-offs.

coreConcepts.name: Data Governance
coreConcepts.definition: Establishing schema review processes, naming conventions, and approval gates.

coreConcepts.name: Replication & HA
coreConcepts.definition: Designing high-availability architectures with replication, failover, and disaster recovery.

coreConcepts.name: Data Contracts
coreConcepts.definition: Defining cross-service data contracts that decouple teams while maintaining data integrity.

coreConcepts.name: Capacity Planning
coreConcepts.definition: Forecasting storage, throughput, and compute requirements for database infrastructure.

coreSkills.name: Architecture Design
coreSkills.definition: Can design data architectures that scale across teams and services.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Produces architecture documents with clear trade-off analysis.

coreSkills.name: Governance Establishment
coreSkills.definition: Can establish and enforce data governance practices.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Implements schema review processes with clear approval criteria.

coreSkills.name: HA/DR Design
coreSkills.definition: Can design high-availability and disaster recovery strategies.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Defines RPO/RTO targets and tests failover procedures.

coreSkills.name: Cross-Team Alignment
coreSkills.definition: Can align multiple teams on shared data models and contracts.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Leads data model review sessions across service boundaries.

coreSkills.name: Technology Evaluation
coreSkills.definition: Can evaluate database technologies against requirements.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Produces decision documents comparing SQL and NoSQL options for a given workload.

observableBehaviors: Establishes schema review processes with clear approval gates.
Designs data models that are stable under changing access patterns.
Treats backward compatibility of schema changes as a first-class constraint.
Leads cross-team alignment on shared reference data.
failureModes: Designs theoretical architectures disconnected from practical team constraints.
Imposes governance overhead that slows teams without proportionate benefit.
Chooses distributed systems when a single well-tuned database would suffice.
Neglects backward compatibility in schema evolution.

graduationCriteria.text: This is the terminal level — mastery is demonstrated through sustained organizational impact.
Data architectures designed remain stable through multiple product cycles.
Teams independently follow governance standards without constant enforcement.
Technology decisions are validated by production outcomes over time.

evaluationTasks.title: Design a Multi-Service Data Architecture
evaluationTasks.description: Design the data architecture for a system with 5+ services that share customer and transaction data. Address consistency, ownership, and migration strategy.
successCriteria: Clear data ownership boundaries between services.
Consistency strategy documented with trade-off analysis.
Migration path from current state to target architecture.

learningEmphasis: Learn from post-mortems of data architecture failures.
Practice cross-team communication and governance design.
